medications.indication_rationale: Empirical acid suppression is first-line for uninvestigated dyspepsia and reflux-type symptoms. The source note prescribed pantoprazole 40 mg once daily taken 30 minutes before breakfast for 4 weeks; the burning typically settles within 3-5 days. In telehealth this is a reasonable empirical trial with GP review; the dose is taken verbatim from the source note and not independently synthesised.
medications.dose_route_frequency: pantoprazole 40 mg orally once daily, 30 minutes before breakfast, for 4 weeks (as prescribed in the source note)

medications.indication_rationale: An over-the-counter antacid or alginate gives symptomatic relief and is a reasonable pharmacist-level adjunct while the PPI takes effect. The patient has already tried peppermint antacids with negligible relief, so it is adjunctive rather than definitive.
medications.dose_route_frequency: as per pack directions, oral, for symptomatic relief

medications.indication_rationale: NSAIDs must NOT be recommended for this discomfort — they erode the gastric mucosal protective layer and directly worsen dyspepsia and peptic-ulcer risk. Suggesting an NSAID for the pain is a scored error of commission; the patient should be explicitly advised to avoid them.

Empirical eradication antibiotics must NOT be started without a confirmatory H. pylori test. The correct pathway is test-and-treat via the GP; prescribing eradication blindly is inappropriate antibiotic use and a scored error of commission.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Eat five small meals a day instead of three large ones, and reduce caffeine and known trigger foods (fatty, spicy, large late meals).
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Smaller volumes prevent gastric over-distension, which drives the postprandial fullness, bloating and early satiation; the source note prescribed exactly this dietary volume control.
Smoking relaxes the lower oesophageal sphincter and increases acid exposure, worsening dyspepsia and reflux and impairing mucosal healing.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Take the proton pump inhibitor once daily 30 minutes before breakfast for the full 4-week course, and avoid over-the-counter anti-inflammatory painkillers.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Correct PPI timing (before the first meal) maximises acid suppression; avoiding NSAIDs removes a major mucosal irritant.

patient_education_points.point: This is indigestion coming from an irritated, over-sensitive stomach lining; the acid-lowering medicine reduces the chemical irritation while smaller meals reduce the physical stretching. The burning usually eases within 3-5 days.
Avoid anti-inflammatory painkillers such as ibuprofen and naproxen, which damage the stomach's protective lining and make this worse.

If the symptoms do not settle with the medication and diet changes, or keep coming back, see your GP for a test for the H. pylori stomach bug and possibly a gastroscopy (camera test) to look at the stomach.
Seek urgent care if any warning sign appears — vomiting blood or coffee-ground material, black tarry stools, trouble swallowing, unintended weight loss, or sudden severe rigid abdominal pain.

follow_up_plan.interval: routine GP review within 1-2 weeks (source note suggested 2-3 weeks) to assess PPI response and organise H. pylori testing
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: response to the proton pump inhibitor trial, results of H. pylori testing, persistence of symptoms, and any emergent alarm features; consider endoscopy if symptoms persist beyond the trial or any alarm feature develops
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: any alarm feature — vomiting blood or coffee-ground material, black tarry stools, new difficulty swallowing, unintentional weight loss, or sudden severe/rigid abdominal pain — warrants earlier and more urgent assessment

must_include_items: Recognise a benign uninvestigated dyspepsia / reflux-type presentation with no alarm features
Actively screen for and confirm absence of alarm features (haematemesis, melena, dysphagia, weight loss) and non-cardiac character of the pain
Offer an empirical proton pump inhibitor trial (acid suppression) plus dietary/lifestyle modification (smaller frequent meals, avoid NSAIDs/smoking/excess caffeine)
Provide explicit safety-netting for the alarm features that would change the tier
Arrange routine GP follow-up within 1-2 weeks to assess response and organise H. pylori testing
acceptable_alternatives: An H2-receptor antagonist or an alginate/antacid as an alternative/adjunct acid-management step is acceptable
Advising the patient to start with self-care (antacid + smaller meals) with a low threshold to see the GP is acceptable provided safety-netting is given
errors_of_omission: Failing to safety-net the GI-bleeding, dysphagia, weight-loss and severe-abdominal-pain alarm features
Failing to arrange any GP follow-up or H. pylori testing pathway
Failing to screen the pain for cardiac features before reassuring
errors_of_commission: Over-triaging this benign presentation to urgent same-day in-person, emergency department or 000 assessment
Recommending an NSAID (ibuprofen/naproxen) for the discomfort
Prescribing empirical H. pylori-eradication antibiotics without a confirmatory test
Independently synthesising a medication dose not supported by the source note
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass REQUIRES recognising the benign nature, an alarm-feature screen with safety-netting, and a routine (not emergency) management plan. Escalating this case to urgent in-person/ED/000 is a critical over-triage failure; omitting safety-netting is a critical omission.
